Common student error: subtracting magnitudes to get 2 −2/4 (negative fraction in mixed number) instead of borrowing. Strategy: when the second fraction is bigger, BORROW 1 from the whole-number part and convert it to the denominator (1 = 4/4) so subtraction works without negatives.

questions.prompt: 10^3 in standard form.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 10^3 in standard form (5.NBT.A.2). 10^3 = 10 x 10 x 10 = 1,000. Quick rule: 10 to the n-th power = 1 followed by n zeros. So 10^3 = 1,000 (three zeros). Common student error: writing 30 (multiplying 10 x 3) or 100 (using 10^2). Strategy: memorize powers of 10 — 10^1 = 10, 10^2 = 100, 10^3 = 1,000, 10^4 = 10,000, 10^5 = 100,000. NYS Grade 5 5.NBT.A.2 expects exponential ↔ standard fluency.

questions.prompt: The product 25 x 14.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 25 x 14 using the standard algorithm (5.NBT.B.5). Partial 1: 25 x 4 = 100. Partial 2: 25 x 10 = 250. Sum: 100 + 250 = 350. Common student error: forgetting the 0 placeholder for the tens row (100 + 25 = 125) or making basic-fact errors. Strategy: use the 25 trick if helpful: 25 x 14 = (100/4) x 14 = 1,400/4 = 350. For standard algorithm, never forget the 0 placeholder when moving from the ones row to the tens row.

questions.prompt: Volume of a rectangular prism 3 in by 4 in by 5 in.
questions.explanation: Worked example: rectangular prism 3 x 4 x 5 inches — volume (5.MD.C.5). V = l x w x h = 3 x 4 x 5 = 60 cubic inches. Common student error: adding (3+4+5=12) or only multiplying two dimensions (12 in² instead of 60 in³). Strategy: ALL three dimensions must be multiplied for volume. Units are CUBIC (in³, cm³, etc.). Estimate: a 3x4x5 prism holds 60 unit cubes (3 long, 4 wide, 5 high). NYS Grade 5 5.MD.C.5 expects unit-cube reasoning underlying the formula.

questions.prompt: The quotient 504 ÷ 7.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 504 ÷ 7 using long division (5.NBT.B.6). 7 doesn't go into 5, so consider 50. 7 x 7 = 49; remainder 1. Bring down the 4 → 14. 7 x 2 = 14; remainder 0. Quotient = 72. Check: 7 x 72 = 504. Common student error: starting with 5 ÷ 7 = 0 and writing 072 (correct value with leading zero) or making subtraction errors. Strategy: standard DMSB cycle — Divide, Multiply, Subtract, Bring down. Repeat until no digits remain to bring down. Always verify by multiplication.

questions.prompt: Volume of a composite figure made of one prism with V = 18 cm^3 and another with V = 24 cm^3.
questions.explanation: Worked example: composite figure of two prisms with volumes 18 cm³ and 24 cm³ (5.MD.C.5c). Add the volumes: 18 + 24 = 42 cm³. Common student error: multiplying volumes or treating the composite as a single rectangular prism. Strategy: composite (irregular) figures are decomposed into rectangular prism parts. Find each part's volume separately, then ADD them. NYS Grade 5 5.MD.C.5c tests this additive volume principle directly using L-shaped or T-shaped solids.

questions.prompt: Side length of a cube with volume 125 cubic feet.
questions.explanation: Worked example: cube with volume 125 ft³ — find side length (5.MD.C.5). V = s³ where s is side length. We need s³ = 125. Test small cubes: 4³ = 64, 5³ = 125 ✓. So s = 5 feet. Common student error: dividing 125 by 3 to get 41.67 or by 6 (sum of cube faces) to get 20.83. Strategy: 'side length from cube volume' means take the CUBE ROOT. Memorize 1³=1, 2³=8, 3³=27, 4³=64, 5³=125, 6³=216, 10³=1,000. NYS Grade 5 5.MD.C.5 uses cube volume to introduce the inverse relationship.

questions.prompt: The y-coordinate of (4, 9).
questions.explanation: Worked example: in (4, 9) the y-coordinate is 9 (5.G.A.1). The y-coordinate is the SECOND number in the ordered pair (x, y). Common student error: writing 4 (x-coordinate) instead. Strategy: ordered pairs are written (x, y) — x comes first, y comes second. The y-coordinate tells you how far UP (positive) or DOWN (negative) from the x-axis. NYS Grade 5 5.G.A.1 expects fluent reading and writing of ordered pairs in the first quadrant.

questions.prompt: Coordinates of a point 5 right and 0 up from origin.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 5 right and 0 up from origin = (5, 0) (5.G.A.1). 5 right means x = 5; 0 up means y = 0. So the point is (5, 0) — on the positive x-axis. Common student error: writing (0, 5) (swap) or (5). Strategy: ordered pair = (x, y), always with two numbers. Any point ON the x-axis has y-coordinate = 0. Any point ON the y-axis has x-coordinate = 0. NYS Grade 5 5.G.A.1 tests these boundary cases as part of plotting fluency.

questions.prompt: Equation for points (0,0), (1, 4), (2, 8).
questions.explanation: Worked example: (0,0), (1,4), (2,8) — find the equation (5.OA.B.3). Notice each y is 4 times its x: 0=4·0, 4=4·1, 8=4·2. So y = 4x. Common student error: writing y = x + 4 (matches (0,0)? No: 0+4=4≠0). Strategy: proportional relationships pass through the origin AND have the form y = kx where k = y/x is constant. Test EVERY point against your rule. NYS Grade 5 5.OA.B.3 introduces this proportional structure as a bridge to Grade 6 ratios.

questions.prompt: The quotient 1,326 ÷ 17.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 1,326 ÷ 17 (5.NBT.B.6). Estimate: 17 x 80 = 1,360; close. Try 17 x 78 = 1,326 ✓. So quotient = 78, remainder 0. Long division: 17 doesn't go into 1; consider 13 → no; consider 132. 17 x 7 = 119; remainder 13. Bring down 6 → 136. 17 x 8 = 136; remainder 0. Quotient = 78. Common student error: poor estimation leading to off-by-one quotient digits. Strategy: estimate first by rounding (17 ≈ 20, 1,326 ≈ 1,400; 1,400 ÷ 20 = 70). Adjust as you compute.

questions.prompt: Term in pattern B if pattern A is 6 and rule is 'B = 3 times A.'.
questions.explanation: Worked example: if A = 6 and B = 3 x A, then B = 3 x 6 = 18 (5.OA.B.3). Use the rule 'B = 3 times A' as a multiplication: substitute A = 6, multiply by 3. Common student error: writing 9 (adding instead) or 2 (dividing). Strategy: when a rule says 'B is k times A,' it means B = k x A. Substitute the given A value and multiply. This rule produces a proportional pattern: (A, B) pairs lie on the line y = 3x.
